  • Module: Image Agent  agent implementation

  • Admin: Scaling  GPU considerations

docs.title: Local Development
docs.text: Set up Memex for local development and testing.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+

  • Docker Desktop with GPU support

  • Git

  • NVIDIA drivers (for GPU inference)

docs.title: Clone and Setup
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/your-org/Agent_Swarm.git

cd Agent_Swarm



# Create virtual environment

python -m venv .venv

source .venv/bin/activate  # Linux/Mac

# or: .venv\Scripts\activate  # Windows



# Install dependencies

pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Start Ollama
docs.text:
# Pull required models

docker run -d --gpus all -p 11434:11434 \

    --name ollama -v ollama_data:/root/.ollama \

    ollama/ollama



docker exec ollama ollama pull qwen3.6:27b

docker exec ollama ollama pull nemotron-orchestrator:8b

docker exec ollama ollama pull llama-guard-3:8b

docs.title: Configure Environment
docs.text:
cp network.env.example network.env

Edit network.env for local development:

LOVELACE_IP=127.0.0.1

HOPPER_IP=127.0.0.1

TURING_IP=127.0.0.1

OLLAMA_HOST=http://localhost:11434

AGENT_RUNTIME_PORT=8008

docs.title: Run Agent Runtime
docs.text:
cd agents

python main.py

The runtime starts at http://localhost:8008.

docs.title: Test Your Changes
docs.text:
# Run all tests

pytest



# Run specific test

pytest tests/test_valid.py



# Quick health check

curl http://localhost:8008/

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
agents/

+-- main.py                    # FastAPI app entry point

+-- router.py                  # Request handling, dispatch

+-- semantic_router.py         # Intent classification

+-- mars_loop.py              # MarsRL verification pipeline

+-- coordinator.py            # Multi-agent orchestration

+-- config.py                 # Configuration loading

+-- security/                 # SPIFFE, JWT-ACE, auth middleware

+-- specialized/              # Image, voice, IoT, 3D agents

+-- expertise/                # Template registry, A/B testing

+-- tools/                    # Agent tools (file_ops, terminal, etc.)

docs.title: Development Workflow
docs.text:
1. Create a feature branch: git checkout -b feature/my-change

  2. Make changes to agent code in agents/

  3. Test locally with pytest and manual curl requests

  4. Check logs in logs/ directory

  5. Submit a pull request

docs.title: Enable Debug Logging
docs.text: LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG python agents/main.py

docs.title: View Langfuse Traces Locally
docs.text:
If running Langfuse locally:

docker compose -f control_plane/docker-compose.yml up -d langfuse clickhouse postgres

Access at http://localhost:3000.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Getting Started: Developer Quickstart  condensed setup

  • Developer: Adding Agents  extend the agent system

docs.title: Chat Completions API
docs.text: The primary API for interacting with Memex. OpenAI-compatible format.

docs.title: Endpoint
docs.text:
POST /v1/chat/completions

Via Gateway: POST http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/chat/completions

docs.title: Headers
docs.text: Header Required Value Content-Type Yes application/json

docs.title: Body
docs.text:
{
    "messages": [
        {"role": "system", "content": "Optional system prompt"},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Your message here"}
    ],
    "stream": true,
    "model": "qwen3.6:27b",
    "session_id": "optional-session-id",
    "owner_id": "optional-user-id",
    "dev_mode": false,
    "temperature": 0.7,
    "max_tokens": 4096
}

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description messages array required Chat messages (role + content) stream bool true Enable SSE streaming model string qwen3.6:27b Model override session_id string auto-generated Conversation session ID owner_id string "default" User identifier dev_mode bool false Enable developer tools temperature float 0.7 Sampling temperature max_tokens int 4096 Maximum response tokens

docs.title: Response (Non-Streaming)
docs.text:
{
    "id": "chatcmpl-abc123",
    "object": "chat.completion",
    "created": 1712764800,
    "model": "qwen3.6:27b",
    "choices": [
        {
            "index": 0,
            "message": {
                "role": "assistant",
                "content": "Here's your response..."
            },
            "finish_reason": "stop"
        }
    ],
    "usage": {
        "prompt_tokens": 50,
        "completion_tokens": 200,
        "total_tokens": 250
    },
    "metadata": {
        "intent": "CODE",
        "confidence": 0.92,
        "mars_score": 0.85,
        "trace_id": "trace-xyz789"
    }
}

docs.title: Response (Streaming)
docs.text:
With "stream": true, the response is Server-Sent Events:

data: {"event": "status", "data": "Classifying intent..."}

data: {"event": "status", "data": "Intent: CODE (0.92)"}

data: {"event": "thought", "data": "Analyzing the request..."}

data: {"event": "response", "data": "Here's the code:\n```python\n..."}

data: {"event": "response", "data": "```\n"}

data: [DONE]

docs.title: Event Types
docs.text: Event Description status Progress updates (intent classification, verification) thought Agent reasoning (visible in debug mode) response Content tokens tool_call Tool invocation details error Error information

docs.title: cURL
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Write a Python fibonacci function"}],
        "stream": false
    }'

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
import requests

response = requests.post(
    "http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/chat/completions",
    json={
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Write a Python fibonacci function"}],
        "stream": False,
    },
)
print(response.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"])

docs.title: Python (Streaming)
docs.text:
import requests

with requests.post(
    "http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/chat/completions",
    json={
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Explain Docker"}],
        "stream": True,
    },
    stream=True,
) as resp:
    for line in resp.iter_lines():
        if line:
            print(line.decode())

docs.title: Error Responses
Governance API
docs.text: Submit and manage governance requests for privileged operations.

docs.title: Submit Request
docs.text: POST /v1/governance/request

docs.title: Body
docs.text:
{
    "action": "execute_command",
    "command": "rm -rf /tmp/old-data",
    "reason": "Cleaning up stale temporary files",
    "session_id": "session-abc",
    "owner_id": "user_001"
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
    "request_id": "gov-xyz789",
    "status": "pending_review",
    "action": "execute_command",
    "created_at": "2026-04-10T14:30:00Z"
}

docs.title: Get Request Status
docs.text: GET /v1/governance/{request_id}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
    "request_id": "gov-xyz789",
    "status": "approved",
    "action": "execute_command",
    "reviewer": "admin",
    "reviewed_at": "2026-04-10T14:35:00Z",
    "result": "Command executed successfully"
}

docs.title: Governance Statuses
docs.text: Status Description pending_review Awaiting admin review approved Approved and executed denied Rejected by admin auto_approved Approved by policy (low-risk) expired Not reviewed within timeout

docs.title: List Pending Requests
docs.text:
GET /v1/governance/pending

Returns all requests in pending_review status.

docs.title: Approve / Deny
docs.text:
POST /v1/governance/{request_id}/approve
POST /v1/governance/{request_id}/deny

docs.title: Body (Deny)
docs.text:
{
    "reason": "Too risky — use a safer alternative"
}

docs.title: Grounding API
docs.text: Endpoints for checking and requesting grounding permissions, and for managing the document knowledge library used by Doc Grounding.

docs.title: Check Grounding Status
docs.text:
Returns the current grounding permissions for the authenticated user.

GET /api/v1/grounding/status

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "owner_id": "user_001",
  "web_grounding": true,
  "docs_grounding": false
}
Field Type Description owner_id string The resolved owner identifier for the session web_grounding bool Whether internet/web grounding is permitted docs_grounding bool Whether knowledge-base document grounding is permitted

docs.title: Request Grounding Permission
docs.text:
Submits a governance request to unlock a grounding capability. The request is reviewed by an admin; on approval the permission is automatically written to the grounding store.

POST /api/v1/grounding/request

docs.title: Body
docs.text:
{
  "permission": "web_grounding",
  "reason": "Need live search results for research workflows"
}
Field Type Required Description permission string ✅ "web_grounding" or "docs_grounding" reason string — Human-readable justification shown to the admin reviewer

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "status": "submitted",
  "request_id": "a1b2c3d4",
  "permission": "web_grounding"
}

The returned request_id maps to a standard governance request. Track it via GET /api/v1/request/{id}.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: HTTP Meaning 400 permission value is not "web_grounding" or "docs_grounding" 500 Governance request could not be persisted

docs.title: Using Grounding in Chat
docs.text:
Pass grounding_web and/or grounding_docs as boolean fields in the Chat Completions request body. These are ignored (treated as false) if the user does not have the corresponding permission.

{
  "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is the latest Python version?"}],
  "model": "default",
  "stream": true,
  "grounding_web": true,
  "grounding_docs": false
}

docs.title: Behaviour
docs.text: Field Value Result grounding_web true + permission granted + intent matches DuckDuckGo results injected as [Web Grounding Context] system message grounding_web true + permission granted + no keyword match Search skipped; model answers from its own knowledge grounding_web true + no permission Status event emitted: "⚠️ Web grounding not permitted"; request continues without grounding grounding_docs true + permission granted + chunks found PgVector results injected as [Document Context] system message grounding_docs true + permission granted + no chunks Status event: "→ Doc grounding: no relevant chunks found" grounding_docs true + no permission Status event emitted; request continues without grounding

docs.title: Knowledge Ingest
docs.text: Add documents to the persistent knowledge library used by Doc Grounding.

docs.title: Ingest Text
docs.text: POST /api/v1/knowledge/ingest

docs.title: Body
docs.text:
{
  "content": "The authentication gateway uses SPIRE SVID tokens for inter-service auth.",
  "source": "architecture-notes",
  "tags": ["auth", "spire", "architecture"]
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "status": "accepted"
}

Note

The ingest endpoint accepts content and queues it for embedding. Documents are stored in the architect_knowledge PgVector table and become available for Doc Grounding retrieval immediately after embedding completes.

docs.title: Ingest File
docs.text:
POST /api/v1/knowledge/ingest_file

Upload a file (PDF, Markdown, plain text) as a multipart form:

curl -X POST http://192.168.2.103/api/v1/knowledge/ingest_file \
  -F "file=@project-spec.pdf" \
  -F "source=project-spec.pdf"

docs.title: Admin: Manual Permission Grant / Revoke
docs.text: Admins can manage permissions directly without the governance workflow.

docs.title: Grant
docs.text:
from grounding_permissions import grounding_permissions

grounding_permissions.grant("owner_id", "web_grounding")
grounding_permissions.grant("owner_id", "docs_grounding")

docs.title: Revoke
docs.text: grounding_permissions.revoke("owner_id", "web_grounding")

docs.title: Persistence
docs.text: Permissions are stored at /workspace/grounding_permissions.json on the control-plane host. The store is reloaded on each request; no service restart is required after manual edits.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Chat Completions API — full chat request schema
  • Governance API — approve/reject pending grounding requests
  • User Guide: Grounding — end-user walkthrough

docs.title: Internal API
docs.text: Health, metrics, and debugging endpoints.

docs.title: Health Check
docs.text:
GET /

{
    "status": "ok",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "uptime": 86400
}

docs.title: Metrics (jacquard)
docs.text:
GET /metrics

Returns jacquard-formatted metrics:

# HELP agent_state Current agent state
# TYPE agent_state gauge
agent_state{state="idle"} 1

# HELP workflow_steps_total Total workflow steps
# TYPE workflow_steps_total counter
workflow_steps_total{step="solver"} 1234

# HELP http_request_duration_seconds Request latency
# TYPE http_request_duration_seconds histogram
http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.5"} 2000

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
POST /log

Submit a log event:

{
    "level": "info",
    "message": "Custom event",
    "metadata": {"source": "external"}
}

docs.title: Debug Endpoints
docs.text:
Debug Only

These endpoints are intended for development and should be restricted in production.

docs.title: Config Dump
docs.text:
GET /debug/config

Returns current runtime configuration (secrets redacted).

docs.title: Active Sessions
docs.text:
GET /debug/sessions

Lists active conversation sessions with metadata.

docs.title: Model Status
docs.text:
GET /debug/models

Returns loaded model information from Ollama, including VRAM usage.

docs.title: MemPalace API
docs.text:
Complete reference for the MemPalace HTTP API and MCP tool surface. The service exposes 15 REST endpoints under /v1/... and 4 MCP tools under /mcp. Both surfaces share the same Postgres backend and behave consistently — the MCP tools are designed for agent contexts (terse string output, owner_id always required), the HTTP endpoints for everything else.

Source of truth: control_plane/mempalace/app/main.py

docs.title: Conventions
docs.text: Topic Detail Base URL http://192.168.2.102:8200 Content-Type application/json for all requests with bodies Auth None at the network layer (relies on private LAN). actor_id is a self-declared audit field — see "Identity & Trust" below Errors FastAPI standard {"detail": "<message>"} with appropriate HTTP status Datetimes All ISO-8601 with timezone (+00:00) IDs UUIDs as strings

docs.title: Identity & Trust
docs.text:
The actor_id query parameter on PATCH /v1/memories/{id} and DELETE /v1/memories/{id} is recorded into the audit log but is not authenticated — any caller may declare any string. actor_role is hardcoded server-side to "user" to prevent self-promotion to admin via query string.

Until proper authenticated identity is wired in, the audit trail is best treated as forensic-quality only when the calling agent is itself trusted.

docs.title: Memory taxonomy
docs.text:
Every semantic memory has memory_type and domain:

memory_type Hall mapping Meaning semantic hall_facts Factual knowledge (default) episodic hall_events Events / experiences procedural hall_advice Rules / how-to preference hall_preferences User preference discovery hall_discoveries Findings / insights

domain is free-form (coding / visual / architecture / general / …). The Palace UI surfaces unique domain values as "rooms".

docs.title: GET /health
docs.text:
Liveness check. Performs a SELECT 1 against Postgres so a 200 confirms both the FastAPI process and DB connectivity. Used by the Docker HEALTHCHECK.

Response 200

{"status": "ok", "service": "mempalace"}

docs.title: POST /v1/memories — Store
docs.text:
Stores a single memory. Generates the embedding via Ollama (nomic-embed-text) and writes a created audit row in the same transaction.

Query

Param Type Required Notes actor_id string optional Recorded in audit; defaults to agent_id or owner_id if empty

Body (MemoryCreate)

Field Type Required Default Notes content string ✓ The memory itself memory_type string "semantic" One of the 5 taxonomy values domain string "general" Free-form agent_id string | null null Creating agent team_id string | null null Coordinator team scope owner_id string ✓ User identity — required for all writes metadata object {} Free-form JSON

Response 200 (MemoryOut) — see shape below.

Error 400 when owner_id missing or empty:

{"detail": "owner_id is required for memory writes"}

docs.title: POST /v1/memories/search — Semantic search
docs.text:
Cosine similarity search over the embeddings. As a side effect, increments access_count for each hit (used by the Palace UI heat indicator and the ADR-005 Phase 2 ranking score).

Body (SearchQuery)

Field Type Default Notes query string required Free-text query owner_id string | null null Filter by user agent_id string | null null Filter by agent team_id string | null null Filter by team memory_type string | null null One of the 5 types domain string | null null Free-form limit int 10 Top-N results

Response 200 — array of MemoryOut ordered by ascending cosine distance (closest first), each with a populated score field in [-1.0, 1.0] where 1.0 = identical.

docs.title: PATCH /v1/memories/{memory_id} — Update
docs.text:
Updates content/type/domain/metadata and writes an edited audit row. Re-embeds if content changes.

Path: memory_id (UUID)

Query

Param Type Required Notes actor_id string ✓ Recorded in audit

Body (MemoryUpdate — all fields optional, only changed fields apply)

Field Type content string memory_type string domain string metadata object

Response 200 (MemoryOut).

Error 404 if memory not found.

docs.title: DELETE /v1/memories/{memory_id} — Delete
docs.text:
Deletes the memory and writes a deleted audit row containing previous_content so the deletion is forensically recoverable from the audit trail (the audit row's memory_id is intentionally not a foreign key — it survives the deletion).

Query

Param Type Default Notes actor_id string "anonymous" Recorded in audit

Response 200

{"status": "deleted", "id": "<uuid>"}

Error 404 if memory not found (no audit row written in this case).

docs.title: GET /v1/memories/stats — Counts
docs.text:
Response 200

{
  "total": 162,
  "breakdown": [
    {"type": "semantic", "domain": "general", "count": 41},
    {"type": "procedural", "domain": "coding", "count": 40},
    ...
  ]
}

docs.title: GET /v1/memories/{memory_id}/audit — Audit history
docs.text:
Path: memory_id (UUID)

Response 200 — array of AuditLogOut ordered newest-first:

Field Type Notes id string Audit row UUID memory_id string Original memory UUID (may reference a deleted memory) action string "created" / "edited" / "deleted" actor_id string Self-declared at write time actor_role string Currently always "user" previous_content string | null For edits/deletes new_content string | null For creates/edits changed_fields object Diff of the changed fields created_at string ISO-8601

docs.title: POST /v1/extract — Run extractor + store
docs.text:
Runs the conversation through the LLM extractor (qwen2.5-coder:14b), embeds each extracted fact in batch, and stores them along with an extraction_log audit row — all in a single Postgres transaction.

Body (ExtractionRequest)

Field Type Required Notes conversation string ✓ Up to ~4000 chars sent to LLM (truncated internally) owner_id string ✓ Required agent_id string | null team_id string | null

Response 200 — array of MemoryOut for the stored memories.

Empty array is a valid response — meaning the LLM extractor identified no durable facts. The extraction attempt is still logged.

Error 400 when owner_id missing.

The agent runtime calls this after each model response: agents/main.py:_mempalace_extract_http.

docs.title: Agent Snapshots
docs.text: Versioned per-agent learned state. Race-safe: the writer retries up to 5× on the unique (agent_id, owner_id, version) constraint.

docs.title: POST /v1/snapshots — Save
docs.text:
Body (SnapshotCreate)

Field Type Required agent_id string ✓ owner_id string | null snapshot_data object ✓

Response 200 (SnapshotOut)

{
  "id": "<uuid>",
  "agent_id": "<agent>",
  "owner_id": "<user>",
  "snapshot_data": {...},
  "version": 3,
  "created_at": "2026-05-08T14:48:21+00:00"
}

Error 503 with body {"detail": "Snapshot save contention — retry later"} if the writer can't claim a free version slot after 5 attempts.

docs.title: GET /v1/snapshots/{agent_id} — Latest
docs.text:
Path: agent_id (string)

Query

Param Type owner_id string | null

Response 200 — latest SnapshotOut, or null if none exist.

docs.title: Team Memory
docs.text: Shared key-value store scoped to a coordinator team. Atomic upserts via INSERT … ON CONFLICT (team_id, key) DO UPDATE.

docs.title: POST /v1/team/{team_id} — Upsert
docs.text:
Path: team_id (string)

Body (TeamMemoryCreate)

Field Type Required key string ✓ value string ✓ author_agent string | null

Response 200 (TeamMemoryOut).

docs.title: GET /v1/team/{team_id} — List all
docs.text: Response 200 — array of TeamMemoryOut ordered by created_at ascending.

docs.title: POST /v1/team/{team_id}/search — Semantic search within team
docs.text:
Body (SearchQuery) — only query and limit are used; team is in path.

Response 200 — array of TeamMemoryOut.

docs.title: DELETE /v1/team/{team_id} — Clear
docs.text:
Bulk-clears all memories for the team. Used post-coordination when the team's scratchpad is no longer needed.

Response 200

{"status": "cleared", "team_id": "<id>", "deleted": 17}

docs.title: Palace Viewer
docs.text: The 3D Palace UI uses these endpoints. The hierarchy is computed dynamically from the memories table — there is no separate "palace" storage.

docs.title: GET /v1/palace/layout — Hierarchy
docs.text:
Query

Param Type Notes owner_id string | null Scope to one user agent_id string | null Scope to one agent

Response 200 (PalaceLayoutOut)

{
  "wings": [
    {
      "name": "wing_owner_<owner_id>",
      "halls": [
        {
          "name": "hall_facts",
          "rooms": [
            {"name": "coding", "drawer_count": 18},
            {"name": "general", "drawer_count": 41}
          ]
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "total_memories": 162
}

Wing prefixes encode scope unambiguously (post-2026-05-08):

Prefix Meaning wing_team_<team_id> Team scope wing_agent_<agent_id> Agent scope wing_owner_<owner_id> Owner scope (no agent or team) wing_self Unscoped (no owner / agent / team)

docs.title: GET /v1/palace/room — Memories at a coordinate
docs.text:
Query

Param Type Required wing string ✓ — see prefix table above hall string ✓ — hall_facts / hall_events / hall_advice / etc. room string ✓ — the domain value owner_id string | null optional extra filter limit int (default 100) offset int (default 0)

Response 200 — array of MemoryOut ordered by created_at desc.

docs.title: GET /v1/palace/audit/extractions — Per-owner extraction stats
docs.text:
Single-query summary used by the Palace audit panel.

Response 200

[
  {
    "owner_id": "62e65d4d...",
    "owner_id_short": "62e65d4d...",
    "total_attempts": 39,
    "total_memories_stored": 151,
    "current_memory_count": 154,
    "success_rate": 0.8974,
    "last_attempt_at": "2026-05-08T00:28:48+00:00",
    "last_success_at": "2026-05-08T00:28:48+00:00"
  }
]
Field Meaning total_attempts Number of /v1/extract calls for this owner total_memories_stored Sum of memories stored across all attempts current_memory_count Live row count in memories table for this owner success_rate Attempts that produced ≥1 memory ÷ total attempts

current_memory_count may differ from total_memories_stored because memories can be deleted via DELETE /v1/memories/{id} after they were originally stored.

docs.title: MCP Tools
docs.text:
Mounted at /mcp (Streamable HTTP transport). All four tools require owner_id to be a non-empty string — without it they short-circuit and return an error string rather than performing the operation. This matches the HTTP owner_id requirement and prevents agents from creating orphan memories or reading across user boundaries.

Transport security restricts allowed hosts to 192.168.2.102:*, localhost:*, 127.0.0.1:*, and hopper:*. DNS rebinding protection is enabled.

docs.title: search_memories_mcp
docs.text:
search_memories_mcp(query: str, owner_id: str, agent_id: str = "", limit: int = 10) -> str

Returns a newline-joined string of results, one per line:

[0.8743] (semantic/coding) Always use type hints in Python code
[0.8124] (procedural/coding) Prefer dataclasses over plain dicts

Or "No memories found." if empty. Or "Error: owner_id is required ..." if owner_id blank.

docs.title: store_memory_mcp
docs.text:
store_memory_mcp(
    content: str,
    memory_type: str = "semantic",
    domain: str = "general",
    agent_id: str = "",
    owner_id: str,
) -> str

Returns "Stored memory <uuid> [<type>/<domain>]" on success, or "Error: owner_id is required for memory writes" if blank.

docs.title: get_memory_stats_mcp
docs.text:
get_memory_stats_mcp() -> str

Returns a multi-line summary:

Total: 162
  semantic/coding: 18
  procedural/coding: 40
  ...

docs.title: extract_from_conversation_mcp
docs.text:
extract_from_conversation_mcp(
    conversation: str,
    owner_id: str = "",
    agent_id: str = "",
    domain: str = "general",
) -> str

Returns a multi-line summary of stored memories with truncated content previews:

Stored 3 memories:
  - User prefers cyberpunk visual style with neon colors and rain…
  - Python code should always use type hints and follow PEP-8…
  - When deploying mempalace, stamp baseline before first boot…

Or "No memories extracted from conversation." if the LLM identified none.

docs.title: MemoryOut shape
docs.text:
The canonical "stored memory" representation, used by every endpoint that returns a memory:

Field Type Notes id string UUID content string The memory memory_type string One of 5 taxonomy values domain string | null agent_id string | null team_id string | null owner_id string | null metadata object Free-form created_at string ISO-8601 access_count int Bumped on every search hit score float | null Populated only on search responses wing / hall / room string | null Populated from metadata if present

docs.title: Status codes
docs.text: Code Used by Meaning 200 All success paths 400 POST /v1/memories, POST /v1/extract Missing owner_id 404 PATCH/DELETE /v1/memories/{id} Memory not found 422 PATCH /v1/memories/{id} Missing required actor_id query param 500 Any Ollama unreachable, unhandled error 503 POST /v1/snapshots Version-claim contention exhausted retries

docs.title: Store a memory
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://192.168.2.102:8200/v1/memories \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "content": "User prefers cyberpunk aesthetic with neon and rain",
    "memory_type": "preference",
    "domain": "visual",
    "owner_id": "user_001"
  }'

docs.title: Search
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://192.168.2.102:8200/v1/memories/search \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "query": "what visual style does the user like?",
    "owner_id": "user_001",
    "memory_type": "preference",
    "limit": 5
  }'

docs.title: Extract from a conversation
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://192.168.2.102:8200/v1/extract \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "conversation": "User asked for help with Python error handling. Assistant noted user prefers concise responses with code examples.",
    "owner_id": "user_001",
    "agent_id": "code_developer"
  }'

docs.title: Save a snapshot
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://192.168.2.102:8200/v1/snapshots \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "agent_id": "architect",
    "owner_id": "user_001",
    "snapshot_data": {
      "learned_rules": ["prefer alpine images", "always use type hints"]
    }
  }'

docs.title: Edit a memory (with audit)
docs.text:
curl -X PATCH "http://192.168.2.102:8200/v1/memories/<uuid>?actor_id=user_001" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"content": "Updated memory text"}'

curl http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/models | python -m json.tool

This endpoint proxies to the Ollama /api/tags endpoint and transforms the response to OpenAI format.

docs.title: Tasks API
docs.text: Manage async tasks (image generation, 3D rendering, long-running operations).

docs.title: Get Task Status
docs.text: GET /v1/tasks/{task_id}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
    "task_id": "task-abc123",
    "status": "completed",
    "intent": "IMAGE",
    "created_at": "2026-04-10T14:30:00Z",
    "completed_at": "2026-04-10T14:30:45Z",

        "image_path": "/delivered_artifacts/images/img_20260410.png",
        "metadata": {
            "prompt": "sunset over mountains",
            "steps": 20,
            "seed": 42
        }
    }
}

docs.title: Task Statuses
docs.text: Status Description queued Waiting in dispatcher queue processing Currently being executed completed Finished successfully failed Execution error

docs.title: List Active Tasks
docs.text: GET /v1/tasks

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
    "tasks": [
        {
            "task_id": "task-abc123",
            "status": "processing",
            "intent": "IMAGE",
            "created_at": "2026-04-10T14:30:00Z"
        }
    ],
    "total": 1
}

docs.title: Cancel Task
docs.text: DELETE /v1/tasks/{task_id}

docs.title: Response

{
    "task_id": "task-abc123",
    "status": "cancelled"
}

docs.title: Training API
docs.text:
The training pipeline is a distributed, two-node system:

  • Lovelace (192.168.2.101:8001) — Training Dispatcher FastAPI service. Owns the GPU and runs grpo_trainer.py subprocesses.
  • Turing (agent_runtime) — Submits jobs to the dispatcher via DISPATCHER_URL.

All dispatcher endpoints except /health require the X-Dispatcher-Key header (value = DISPATCHER_SECRET).

docs.title: Health Check
docs.text:
GET http://192.168.2.101:8001/health

No authentication required. Used as a liveness probe.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
    "status": "online",
    "node": "lovelace",
    "ip": "192.168.2.101",
    "active_jobs": 0,
    "total_jobs": 12,
    "available_archetypes": ["coder", "coordinator", "researcher", "creative"]
}

docs.title: Submit Training Job
docs.text:
POST http://192.168.2.101:8001/train
X-Dispatcher-Key: <DISPATCHER_SECRET>

docs.title: Body
docs.text:
{
    "archetype": "coder",
    "dataset_path": "/workspace/training_data/curated.jsonl",
    "base_model": "Qwen/Qwen3-27B",
    "max_seq_len": 2048,
    "force": false,
    "dry_run": false
}
Field Type Required Description archetype string Yes One of coder, coordinator, researcher, creative. Must exist in ARCHETYPE_TRAINING_CONFIGS. dataset_path string No Override default dataset path. base_model string No Override base model from archetype config. max_seq_len int No Max sequence length (default: 2048). force bool No Bypass training time window check. dry_run bool No Run preflight only; do not launch training.

docs.title: Response — Success (202 Accepted)
docs.text:
{
    "job_id": "job-20260504-001",
    "status": "running",
    "archetype": "coder",
    "started_at": "2026-05-04T07:15:00Z"
}

docs.title: Error: Unknown Archetype (422)
docs.text: {"detail": "Unknown archetype 'foo'. Valid: coder, coordinator, researcher, creative"}

docs.title: Error: Job Already Running (409)
docs.text: {"detail": "A training job is already running (job-20260504-001). Cancel it first or wait."}

docs.title: Error: Secret Not Configured (503)
docs.text: {"detail": "Dispatcher secret not configured — service is in locked mode."}

docs.title: Poll Job Status
docs.text:
GET http://192.168.2.101:8001/train/{job_id}
X-Dispatcher-Key: <DISPATCHER_SECRET>

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
    "job_id": "job-20260504-001",
    "archetype": "coder",
    "status": "running",
    "started_at": "2026-05-04T07:15:00Z",
    "finished_at": null,
    "exit_code": null,
    "log_tail": "Epoch 2/3  loss=0.342  reward_mean=0.78 ..."
}

status values: running | completed | failed | cancelled

docs.title: List Jobs
docs.text:
GET http://192.168.2.101:8001/jobs
X-Dispatcher-Key: <DISPATCHER_SECRET>

Returns up to 50 most-recent jobs, newest first.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
[
    {"job_id": "job-20260504-001", "archetype": "coder", "status": "running", ...},
    {"job_id": "job-20260503-002", "archetype": "researcher", "status": "completed", ...}
]

docs.title: Cancel Job
docs.text:
DELETE http://192.168.2.101:8001/train/{job_id}
X-Dispatcher-Key: <DISPATCHER_SECRET>

Sends SIGTERM to the training subprocess. Returns 404 if the job does not exist or has already finished.

docs.title: Response (200)
docs.text: {"job_id": "job-20260504-001", "status": "cancelled"}

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Module: Training Dispatcher
  • Admin: Secrets

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
Browse by category or use the search bar to find answers.

  • General — What is Memex? Who is it for?
  • Models — Which models? How to switch? Performance?
  • Deployment — Hardware, Docker, networking
  • Security — Authentication, SPIRE, secrets
  • Troubleshooting FAQ — Quick fixes for common issues

docs.title: Can I run everything on a single machine?
docs.text: Yes. Merge all Docker Compose files and run all services on one machine. You'll need at least 32 GB RAM and an NVIDIA GPU.

docs.title: Do I need Docker?
docs.text: Yes. All services run as Docker containers managed by Docker Compose.

docs.title: What Linux distribution should I use?
docs.text: Ubuntu 22.04+ or Debian 12+ recommended. Any distribution with Docker Engine and NVIDIA Container Toolkit support will work.

docs.title: Can I run it on Windows or macOS?
docs.text: The Execution Node requires NVIDIA GPU passthrough, which works best on Linux. The Control and Gateway nodes can theoretically run on any Docker-capable OS, but Linux is recommended for all nodes.

docs.title: How do I update?
docs.text:
git pull origin main
docker compose build
docker compose up -d

See Updates.

docs.title: What ports need to be open?
docs.text: Only between your internal nodes. See Port Map for the full list. No ports need to be exposed to the internet.

docs.title: Can I use Kubernetes instead of Docker Compose?
docs.text: Not currently. The deployment is designed around Docker Compose. A Kubernetes migration is possible but not officially supported.

docs.title: How do I back up everything?
docs.text:
See Backup & Restore. Key items:

  • PostgreSQL database dump
  • workspace/ directory
  • network.env configuration
  • SPIRE configuration files

docs.title: What happens if a node goes down?
docs.text:
• Control Node down: Langfuse tracing and PostgreSQL are unavailable, but agents can still respond (with degraded observability)
  • Execution Node down: All inference stops — this is the critical node
  • Gateway Node down: External access is lost, but services still run internally

docs.title: What is Memex?
docs.text: Memex is a self-hosted, multi-agent AI platform that runs on your own hardware. It orchestrates multiple specialized AI agents — for chat, image generation, 3D modeling, voice, IoT control, and more — through a unified interface.

docs.title: Who is it for?
docs.text:
Anyone who wants a private, extensible AI assistant. The three main audiences are:

  • Users — interact via the Hive UI
  • Admins — deploy and manage the infrastructure
  • Developers — extend with custom agents and tools

docs.title: What hardware do I need?
docs.text:
At minimum:

  • One machine with an NVIDIA GPU (8 GB+ VRAM)
  • 32 GB RAM
  • 100 GB storage

The recommended setup uses three nodes (Control, Execution, Gateway). See Prerequisites.

docs.title: Is it free?
docs.text: Yes. Memex is open source. The LLMs run locally via Ollama — no API keys or subscriptions required.

docs.title: What models does it use?
docs.text:
By default:

Role Model Solver qwen3.6:27b Router nemotron-orchestrator:8b Verifier llama-guard-3:8b

You can switch to any Ollama-compatible model. See Switch Models.

docs.title: Can I use it without internet?
docs.text: Yes, once models are downloaded. All inference, storage, and processing happen on your local network. Internet is only needed for web searches in Research Mode.

docs.title: How is it different from ChatGPT/Claude?
docs.text:
• Private — your data never leaves your network
  • Multi-agent — specialized agents for different tasks
  • Self-hosted — runs on your hardware
  • Extensible — add custom agents, tools, and workflows
  • IoT-connected — controls smart home devices

docs.title: Which models are supported?
docs.text:
Any model available through Ollama. Popular choices:

  • Qwen series (2.5, 3.5)  strong general performance

  • Llama series (3.1, 3.3, Guard)  Meta's open models

  • Gemma series  Google's efficient models

  • Mistral/Mixtral  fast inference

  • DeepSeek  coding-focused

  • Nemotron  NVIDIA's optimized models

docs.title: How do I add a new model?
docs.text:
docker exec ollama ollama pull <model-name>

See Add a New Model.

docs.title: Can I use different models for different tasks?
docs.text:
Yes. The system uses three model roles:

  • Solver  handles the main response generation

  • Router  classifies user intent

  • Verifier  checks response quality and safety

Each can be a different model. Configure in network.env.

docs.title: How much VRAM do I need?
docs.text:
| Model Size | VRAM (q4_K_M) | VRAM (FP16) |

|-----------|---------------|-------------|

| 3B | ~2 GB | ~6 GB |

| 79B | ~5 GB | ~18 GB |

| 14B | ~9 GB | ~28 GB |

| 32B | ~20 GB | ~64 GB |

Memex loads up to 3 models by default. Plan VRAM accordingly.

docs.title: Can I use API-based models (OpenAI, Anthropic)?
docs.text: Not out of the box. The system is designed for local Ollama models. However, because it uses the OpenAI-compatible API format, you could in theory point the solver at an external endpoint by changing the API URL configuration.

docs.title: How do I check which models are loaded?
docs.text: curl http://192.168.2.101:11434/api/tags | python -m json.tool

docs.title: Why is inference slow?
docs.text:
See Performance Tuning. Common causes:

  • VRAM pressure (too many models loaded)

  • Large context windows

  • Flash Attention disabled

  • Thermal throttling

docs.title: Is my data private?
docs.text: Yes. All data stays on your local network. No telemetry or data is sent externally. Models run locally via Ollama.

docs.title: How is authentication handled?
docs.text: Memex uses JWT profiles managed through SPIRE for service-to-service authentication. The Hive UI currently operates on the local network without user authentication (it's designed for trusted, private networks).

docs.title: What is SPIRE?
docs.text:
SPIRE (SPIFFE Runtime Environment) provides cryptographic identity to each service. It issues short-lived X.509 certificates (SVIDs) that services use to authenticate with each other over mTLS.

See Security Model.

docs.title: How are secrets managed?
docs.text:
Secrets are stored in network.env and injected as environment variables. For production hardening:

  • Use Docker secrets instead of environment variables
  • Rotate credentials regularly
  • See Secrets Management

docs.title: Can I expose it to the internet?
docs.text:
This is not recommended without additional security measures:

  • Add proper user authentication (OAuth2/OIDC)
  • Enable TLS on all endpoints
  • Set up firewall rules
  • Implement rate limiting
  • Regular security updates

docs.title: Is the Verifier a safety filter?
docs.text: Yes. The MarsRL verifier (llama-guard-3:8b) checks every response for safety, quality, and policy compliance before sending it to the user. Responses that fail verification are regenerated.

docs.title: What about prompt injection?
docs.text:
The system uses several defenses:

  • System prompts are protected from overwrite
  • The Verifier checks for suspicious outputs
  • Tool execution is sandboxed
  • File operations are scoped to the workspace directory

docs.title: Troubleshooting FAQ
docs.text: Quick answers to common issues. For detailed guides, see the Troubleshooting section.

docs.title: The chat doesn't respond
docs.text:
1. Check if the Agent Runtime is running: docker ps | grep agent-runtime
  2. Check if Ollama is healthy: curl http://192.168.2.101:11434/api/tags
  3. Check logs: docker logs agent-runtime --tail 50

docs.title: Image generation fails
docs.text:
1. Verify ComfyUI is running: curl http://192.168.2.101:8188/system_stats
  2. Check GPU memory: docker exec ollama nvidia-smi
  3. Ollama may need to unload models to free VRAM

docs.title: Voice doesn't work
docs.text:
1. Check microphone permissions in the browser
  2. Verify Whisper: curl http://192.168.2.101:9000/health
  3. Verify Piper: curl http://192.168.2.101:5500/health

docs.title: IoT commands don't work
docs.text:
1. Verify Home Assistant connection: check HA_URL and HA_TOKEN in network.env
  2. Test the API directly: curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $HA_TOKEN" http://<ha-ip>:8123/api/

docs.title: "Model not found" errors
docs.text:
The requested model isn't pulled yet:

docker exec ollama ollama pull <model-name>

docs.title: Everything is slow
docs.text:
See Performance Tuning. Quick checks:

  • GPU VRAM usage: nvidia-smi
  • Too many models loaded: reduce OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS
  • Network latency: ping 192.168.2.101

docs.title: How do I check if all services are running?
docs.text:
docker compose ps                # On each node
curl http://192.168.2.103/health   # Gateway health check

docs.title: Where are the logs?
docs.text:
• Docker logs: docker logs <container-name> --tail 100
  • hollerith/knuth: http://192.168.2.103:3001
  • Langfuse traces: http://192.168.2.102:3000
  • Local log files: logs/ directory

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Welcome to Memex. This section helps you get oriented regardless of your role.

docs.title: Choose Your Starting Point
docs.text: Guide Audience What You'll Learn Overview Everyone What Memex is, what it can do, and how it's built Quickstart: Users End users Access the Hive UI, start chatting, explore features Quickstart: Admins System admins Deploy the 3-node cluster from scratch Quickstart: Developers Contributors Clone the repo, run locally, make your first change Core Concepts Everyone Key terms and mental models for understanding the system

docs.title: System at a Glance
docs.text: Property Value Version 3.4 Status Phase 6 Complete Nodes 3 (Control, Execution, Gateway) Primary Model qwen3.6:27b Security SPIFFE/SPIRE + JWT-ACE (MAESTRO L1–L7) External Dependencies None — fully self-hosted

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: Key mental models for understanding Memex.

docs.title: Agents
docs.text:
An agent is a specialized AI worker with a defined role, model, and set of tools. The system has several types:

| Agent | Model | Purpose |

|-------|-------|---------|

| Solver | qwen3.6:27b | Generates initial responses to user requests |

| Router | nemotron-orchestrator:8b | Classifies user intent into one of 14 categories |

| Verifier | llama-guard-3:8b | Validates output safety and correctness |

| Corrector | qwen3.6:27b | Fixes problems identified by the Verifier |

| Coordinator | qwen3:14b | Orchestrates multi-step tasks across workers |

Specialized agents handle domain-specific work: image generation, 3D modeling, voice synthesis, IoT control, and more.

docs.title: Intents
docs.text:
Every user message is classified into an intent by the Semantic Router. The intent determines which agent handles the request and what tools are available.

| Intent | Description | Example |

|--------|-------------|---------|

| CONVERSATION | General chat, factual questions | "What's the capital of France?" |

| CODE | Software engineering, debugging | "Write a Python sort function" |

| DEVOPS | Infrastructure, Docker, CI/CD | "How do I set up nginx?" |

| DATA | SQL, analytics, CSV processing | "Analyze this dataset" |

| IMAGE | 2D visual art, photos | "Generate a sunset painting" |

| 3D | 3D models, meshes | "Create a low-poly tree" |

| ACTION_FIGURE | Posable action figures | "Design a robot action figure" |

| RESEARCH | Deep knowledge synthesis | "Compare React vs Vue" |

| DOCUMENTATION | Writing, formatting, summaries | "Rewrite this README" |

| TRAIN | Teaching the system new rules | "Remember that I prefer dark mode" |

| IOT_CONTROL | Smart home device control | "Turn on the living room lights" |

| IOT_DEV | Firmware, circuits, MQTT | "Write ESP32 code for a sensor" |

| VISION | Analyzing existing images | "What's in this screenshot?" |

| COORDINATE | Complex multi-step tasks | "Build and deploy a web app" |

docs.title: MarsRL Loop
docs.text:
MarsRL (Mars Reinforcement Learning) is the inference-time quality verification loop at the heart of Memex. Every coding request passes through it:


graph LR

    A[User Request] --> B[Solver]

    B -->|Initial Response| C[Verifier]

    C -->|Score = 0.60| D[Pass ?]

    C -->|Score < 0.60| E[Corrector]

    E -->|Fixed Response| C

    C -->|Max 2 iterations| D

docs.title: Verifier Layers
docs.text:
| Layer | What It Checks | Score Penalty | Hard Block? |

|-------|----------------|---------------|-------------|

| AST Parse | Python syntax validity | -0.40 | No |

| Coherence | Non-empty, no repetition loops | -0.25 | No |

| Safety | Content safety (llama-guard-3) | Score ? 0.0 | Yes |

The pass threshold is 0.60. Every interaction is traced in Langfuse with its process-reward score.

docs.title: SPIFFE / SPIRE
docs.text:
SPIFFE (Secure Production Identity Framework for Everyone) provides cryptographic identity to every workload in the system. SPIRE is the runtime that implements SPIFFE.

  • Every Docker container gets an X.509 certificate (SVID) proving its identity

  • Trust domain: home-ai-lab

  • Services authenticate to each other using mutual TLS  no passwords or API keys

  • Example SPIFFE ID: spiffe://home-ai-lab/execution-node

docs.title: JWT-ACE Tokens
docs.text:
JWT-ACE (JSON Web Token  Authorization for Constrained Environments) are ephemeral, per-request capability tokens.

  • Generated when a request is routed to an agent

  • Scoped to the specific intent and tools needed

  • Short-lived  they expire after the request completes

  • Encode the security level required (L1L7)


sequenceDiagram

    participant Router

    participant TokenIssuer

    participant Agent



    Router->>TokenIssuer: Issue token for CODE intent

    TokenIssuer-->>Router: JWT-ACE (tools: file_ops, terminal)

    Router->>Agent: Execute with token

    Agent->>Agent: Verify token scope before each tool call

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
A skill is a registered capability that agents can invoke. Skills are managed by the Skill Registry and exposed via the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

Each skill has:

  • Triggers: Intents, keywords, or regex patterns that activate it

  • Required capabilities: What JWT-ACE permissions it needs

  • Security level: Minimum MAESTRO level required

  • Handler: The function that executes the skill

Examples: read_file, web_search, run_command, get_device_state, create_simulation.

docs.title: Governance
docs.text:
The governance system gates sensitive operations behind an approval workflow. When an agent needs to install a package, use a new model, or escalate permissions, it submits a governance request.


stateDiagram-v2

    [*] --> PENDING: Request submitted

    PENDING --> ASSESSING: Security + architect review

    ASSESSING --> APPROVED: Safe + compatible

    ASSESSING --> REJECTED: Unsafe or incompatible

    APPROVED --> COMPLETED: Action executed

    APPROVED --> FAILED: Execution error

docs.title: Three-Tier Topology
docs.text:
| Tier | Node | Purpose |

|------|------|---------|

| Control Plane | Hopper (192.168.2.102) | Identity, databases, observability, memory |

| Execution Plane | Lovelace (192.168.2.101) | GPU inference, agent runtime, creative tools |

| Gateway | Turing (192.168.2.103) | Reverse proxy, monitoring, secondary inference |

The tiers are connected over a flat LAN (192.168.2.0/24). Traefik on the Gateway routes external traffic to the appropriate backend.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Architecture Deep-Dive  full technical details

  • Data Flow  how a request travels through the system

  • Security Model  SPIFFE, JWT-ACE, MAESTRO in depth

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Memex is a self-hosted, distributed multi-agent AI system that runs entirely on local hardware. No data leaves your network. No external AI APIs are called. Everything  inference, storage, identity, monitoring  runs on three physical machines connected over a local LAN.

docs.title: Intelligent Chat and Coding
docs.text: Every request goes through a MarsRL quality loop: a Solver generates a response, a Verifier validates it (syntax, coherence, safety), and a Corrector fixes problems. The result is higher-quality output than a single model pass.

docs.title: Creative Media
docs.text:
• Image generation using ComfyUI with FLUX and Stable Diffusion XL pipelines

  • 3D model generation using TripoSG and Hunyuan3D

  • Action figure design with articulated joint specifications for 3D printing

docs.title: Voice Interaction
docs.text: BMO  a physical robot character  responds with a cloned voice via RVC synthesis. Text-to-speech is handled by Qwen3-TTS (1.7B parameters) running locally.

docs.title: Smart Home Control
docs.text: Natural language control of Home Assistant devices. Ask the system to turn on lights, check sensor readings, or manage automations. Hardware prototyping is supported through Wokwi ESP32/Arduino simulation.

docs.title: Autonomous Code Execution
docs.text: Sandboxed VS Code environments (OpenHands) for executing code in isolation. The system can write, test, and iterate on code without affecting the host.

docs.title: Architecture at a Glance
docs.text:
The system runs across three nodes:

| Node | Machine | IP | Role |

|------|---------|-----|------|

| Control Plane | Hopper | 192.168.2.102 | Identity (SPIRE), databases (PostgreSQL, ClickHouse), observability (Langfuse), memory (MemPalace) |

| Execution Plane | Lovelace (RTX 5060 Ti 16GB) | 192.168.2.101 | GPU inference (Ollama), Agent Runtime (FastAPI), ComfyUI, Voice Engine, OpenHands |

| Gateway | Dell PowerEdge Turing (RTX 3070 Ti 8GB) | 192.168.2.103 | Reverse proxy (Traefik), monitoring (jacquard, hollerith, knuth), secondary inference |


graph LR

    User([User]) --> Gateway

    Gateway[Gateway · Turing] -->|/swarm/*| Execution[Execution · Lovelace]

    Execution -->|identity| Control[Control · Hopper]

    Execution -->|traces| Control

    Execution -->|memory| Control

    Gateway -->|metrics| Gateway

docs.title: Security Model
docs.text:
• SPIFFE/SPIRE: Every service gets a cryptographic workload identity (X.509 SVID)

  • JWT-ACE: Each request gets an ephemeral capability token scoped to its intent

  • MAESTRO: L1L7 security framework (98% compliant)

  • Output Validation: 3-layer verification  AST parse, coherence heuristics, llama-guard-3 safety check

docs.title: What's Next
docs.text:
• Quickstart for Users  start using the system now

  • Core Concepts  understand the key mental models

  • Architecture Deep-Dive  full technical details

docs.title: Quickstart: Admins
docs.text: Deploy the full Memex stack across three nodes from scratch.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
| Requirement | Details |

|-------------|---------|

| 3 machines | Control Node (low-power x86), Execution Node (NVIDIA GPU = 16GB VRAM), Gateway Node (NVIDIA GPU = 8GB VRAM optional) |

| Network | All nodes on the same subnet with static IPs |

| OS | Ubuntu 22.04+ (Control, Gateway) · Windows 11 or Ubuntu (Execution) |

| Docker | Docker Engine 24+ with Compose v2 on all nodes |

| NVIDIA Drivers | 550+ with NVIDIA Container Toolkit on GPU nodes |

| SSH | Key-based SSH access from your dev machine to all nodes |

docs.title: Network Layout
docs.text:
graph LR

    subgraph CTRL["Control Node<br/>192.168.2.102"]

        C1["SPIRE Server"]

        C2["PostgreSQL"]

        C3["Langfuse"]

        C4["ClickHouse"]

        C5["MemPalace"]

    end



    subgraph EXEC["Execution Node<br/>192.168.2.101"]

        E1["Ollama (GPU)"]

        E2["Agent Runtime"]

        E3["ComfyUI"]

        E4["Voice Engine"]

        E5["OpenHands"]

    end



    subgraph GW["Gateway Node<br/>192.168.2.103"]

        G1["Traefik"]

        G2["jacquard"]

        G3["hollerith"]

        G4["knuth"]

        G5["Ollama (2nd)"]

    end



    CTRL <--> EXEC

    EXEC <--> GW

    CTRL <--> GW

docs.title: Step 1: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
On your development machine:

git clone https://github.com/Misterobots/Agent_Swarm.git

cd Agent_Swarm

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Network
docs.text:
Edit network.env at the repo root with your actual IP addresses:

LOVELACE_IP=192.168.2.101

HOPPER_IP=192.168.2.102

TURING_IP=192.168.2.103

HOME_ASSISTANT_IP=192.168.2.100

Credentials

Update all passwords in network.env before deploying. Never use default passwords in production.

docs.title: Step 3: Deploy Control Plane
docs.text:
SSH to the Control Node and start the services:

ssh ControlPlane

cd ~/Agent_Swarm/control_plane

docker compose up -d

Verify SPIRE server is running:

docker exec spire-server /opt/spire/bin/spire-server healthcheck

docs.title: Step 4: Deploy Execution Plane
docs.text:
On the Execution Node:

cd execution_plane

docker compose up -d

This starts Ollama, Agent Runtime, ComfyUI, Voice Engine, and OpenHands. The first startup will pull LLM models (several GB).

Verify the Agent Runtime is online:

curl http://localhost:8008/

# Expected: {"status": "online", "system": "Home AI Lab Swarm"}

docs.title: Step 5: Deploy Gateway
docs.text:
SSH to the Gateway Node:

ssh Turing

cd ~/Home_AI_Lab/turing_gateway

docker compose up -d

This starts Traefik, jacquard, hollerith, knuth, and the monitoring stack.

docs.title: Step 6: Verify Deployment
docs.text:
Run smoke tests from any node:

# Check Agent Runtime

curl http://192.168.2.101:8008/v1/models



# Check Ollama inference

curl http://192.168.2.101:11434/api/tags



# Check hollerith

curl -s http://192.168.2.103:3001/api/health



# Check Langfuse

curl -s http://192.168.2.102:3000/api/public/health

All Clear

If all four return valid JSON, the cluster is operational. Open http://192.168.2.103 to access the Hive UI.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Post-Deploy Verification  comprehensive health checks

  • Networking Guide  firewall rules, Tailscale, DNS

  • Monitoring Setup  configure hollerith dashboards

  • SPIRE Configuration  workload registration

docs.title: Quickstart: Developers
docs.text: Get the Memex codebase running locally for development.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+

  • Docker Desktop (for dependent services)

  • Git

  • A running Ollama instance (local or remote)

docs.title: Step 1: Clone and Install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Misterobots/Agent_Swarm.git

cd Agent_Swarm

pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Step 2: Configure for Local Development
docs.text:
Create a local override in agents/config.py or set environment variables:

export OLLAMA_HOST=http://localhost:11434

export LLM_PROVIDER=ollama

export LANGFUSE_HOST=http://localhost:3000  # or disable tracing

Minimal Setup

You only need Ollama running locally to work on the agent runtime. The control plane services (SPIRE, Langfuse, PostgreSQL) are optional for development  the runtime falls back gracefully when they're unavailable.

docs.title: Step 3: Pull Required Models
docs.text:
ollama pull qwen3.5:9b                  # Solver / Corrector

ollama pull nemotron-orchestrator:8b     # Router

ollama pull llama-guard-3:8b             # Safety verifier

ollama pull llama3.2:3b                  # Lightweight tasks

docs.title: Step 4: Start the Agent Runtime
docs.text:
cd Agent_Swarm

uvicorn agents.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --log-level debug --reload

The --reload flag enables hot-reloading on code changes.

Verify it's running:

curl http://localhost:8000/

# {"status": "online", "system": "Home AI Lab Swarm"}



curl http://localhost:8000/v1/models

# Returns available model list

docs.title: Step 5: Send a Test Request
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \

  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \

  -d '{

    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Write a Python hello world"}],

    "model": "default",

    "stream": false

  }'

docs.title: Step 6: Run Tests
docs.text: pytest tests/ -v

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
Agent_Swarm/

+-- agents/                  # Core runtime (FastAPI server, agents, routing)

¦   +-- main.py              # FastAPI app, endpoints, lifecycle

¦   +-- router.py            # Intent-based request routing

¦   +-- semantic_router.py   # Intent classifier (14 categories)

¦   +-- mars_loop.py         # MarsRL: Solver ? Verifier ? Corrector

¦   +-- coordinator.py       # Multi-worker orchestration

¦   +-- config.py            # All configuration constants

¦   +-- tools/               # MCP tools (file, terminal, web, IoT)

¦   +-- specialized/         # Domain agents (image, voice, IoT, 3D)

¦   +-- security/            # SPIFFE auth, JWT-ACE, capability gates

¦   +-- training/            # GRPO trainer, A/B testing, datasets

¦   +-- mcp/                 # Model Context Protocol server

+-- control_plane/           # Control Node Docker Compose

+-- execution_plane/         # Execution Node Docker Compose

+-- turing_gateway/            # Gateway Node Docker Compose

+-- ui/                      # Next.js frontend (Hive Mind UI)

+-- services/                # Standalone services (voice_engine, saltbox)

+-- scripts/                 # Deployment and utility scripts

+-- docs-site/               # This documentation site

+-- tests/                   # Test suite

docs.title: Key Files to Know

|------|---------|

| agents/main.py | FastAPI app  all HTTP endpoints, startup lifecycle |

| agents/router.py | Request routing  intent detection ? agent dispatch |

| agents/mars_loop.py | MarsRL quality loop  Solver ? Verifier ? Corrector |

| agents/config.py | Every configuration constant  IPs, models, URLs, thresholds |

| agents/semantic_router.py | Intent classifier  14 categories using nemotron-orchestrator:8b |

docs.title: Quickstart: Users
docs.text: This guide gets you from zero to chatting with the Hive in under five minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• A device on the same network as the Hive (or connected via Tailscale)
  • A modern web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge)

docs.title: Step 1: Open the Hive UI
docs.text:
Navigate to the Hive Mind interface:

http://192.168.2.103

You'll see the main dashboard with a sidebar listing available workspaces.

Remote Access

If you're outside the local network, connect via Tailscale first, then access the gateway node's Tailscale IP.

docs.title: Step 2: Start a Conversation
docs.text:
1. Click Chat in the sidebar
  2. Type a message in the input box and press Enter
  3. Watch the status indicators — you'll see the system route your request, run the MarsRL verification loop, and stream the response

docs.title: What Happens Behind the Scenes
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant You
    participant UI as Hive UI
    participant GW as Gateway (Traefik)
    participant RT as Agent Runtime
    participant LLM as Ollama (Solver)
    participant V as Verifier

    You->>UI: Type message
    UI->>GW: POST /swarm/v1/chat/completions
    GW->>RT: Forward request
    RT->>RT: Classify intent (Router)
    RT->>LLM: Generate response (Solver)
    LLM-->>RT: Initial response
    RT->>V: Verify (AST + Coherence + Safety)
    V-->>RT: Score ≥ 0.60 → Pass
    RT-->>UI: Stream response (SSE)
    UI-->>You: Display response

docs.title: Step 3: Explore Workspaces
docs.text:
The sidebar organizes features into workspaces:

Workspace What It Does Chat General conversation and coding assistance Art Studio Image generation with FLUX and SD-XL Voice Talk to BMO with voice synthesis Tools File operations, web browsing, terminal IoT Control Home Assistant devices Training Monitor and trigger model fine-tuning Governance View and approve capability requests Monitor System health, metrics, logs Settings Preferences, model selection

docs.title: Step 4: Generate an Image
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Art Studio
  2. Enter a description: "A cyberpunk cityscape at night with neon reflections on wet streets"
  3. The router detects the IMAGE intent and delegates to the ComfyUI pipeline
  4. The generated image appears as an artifact card with a download button

docs.title: Step 5: Check System Status
docs.text:
Click Monitor in the sidebar to see:

  • Node health (all three machines)
  • Active model information
  • GPU utilization
  • Recent Langfuse traces

Specialized pipeline for articulated figure design:

  1. Generate T-pose concept art
  2. Create orthographic views (front, side, back)
  3. Reconstruct mesh with joint markers
  4. Define articulation points
  5. Export as rigged .glb

Generated 3D models include an embedded HTML viewer using Three.js:

<!-- Auto-generated viewer -->
<script src="three.min.js"></script>
<script>
    const loader = new GLTFLoader();
    loader.load('model.glb', (gltf) => {
        scene.add(gltf.scene);
    });
</script>

docs.text:
1. Concept Generation: FLUX/SDXL generates T-pose concept art

config = Config()

config.ollama_url       # http://localhost:11434
config.solver_model     # qwen3.6:27b

docs.title: Archetype Training Configs
docs.text:
ARCHETYPE_TRAINING_CONFIGS maps agent archetypes to their training parameters. Used by the Training Dispatcher to validate submitted jobs and resolve dataset/epoch defaults.

ARCHETYPE_TRAINING_CONFIGS: dict = {
    "coder": {
        "datasets": ["glaive-code-assistant", "code-feedback"],
        "epochs": 3,
        "base_model": TRAINING_BASE_SOLVER,
        "description": "Code generation and review"
    },
    "coordinator": {
        "datasets": ["hermes-function-calling", "slim-orca"],
        "epochs": 2,
        "base_model": TRAINING_BASE_SOLVER,
        "description": "Tool use and task delegation"
    },
    "researcher": {
        "datasets": ["openhermes", "slim-orca"],
        "epochs": 2,
        "base_model": TRAINING_BASE_SOLVER,
        "description": "Multi-step reasoning"
    },
    "creative": {
        "datasets": ["openhermes"],
        "epochs": 2,
        "base_model": TRAINING_BASE_SOLVER,
        "description": "Creative writing and ideation"
    },
}
Key datasets epochs coder glaive-code-assistant, code-feedback 3 coordinator hermes-function-calling, slim-orca 2 researcher openhermes, slim-orca 2 creative openhermes 2

The dispatcher exposes the available keys via GET /health as available_archetypes.

docs.title: Training Variables
docs.text: Variable Default Description TRAINING_BASE_SOLVER Qwen/Qwen3-27B Base model for all GRPO fine-tunes TRAINING_NUM_EPOCHS 2 Default epochs (per-archetype overrides this) DISPATCHER_SECRET (set in .env) Shared secret for Training Dispatcher auth DISPATCHER_URL http://192.168.2.101:8001 Dispatcher endpoint (read by agent_runtime) EXPORT_MIN_SCORE 0.85 Minimum MarsRL score for trace export

docs.title: Coordinator
docs.text: Multi-agent orchestration for complex, multi-step tasks.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose agents/coordinator.py Coordinator class — phases and workers

docs.title: When Used
docs.text:
The Coordinator handles COORDINATE and RESEARCH intents — tasks that require:

  • Breaking a problem into subtasks
  • Parallel research or investigation
  • Multiple agent capabilities in one request
  • Verification of the assembled result

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Decompose] --> B[Research]
    B --> C[Synthesize]
    C --> D[Implement]
    D --> E[Verify]
Phase Description Workers Decompose LLM breaks the task into subtasks 1 (coordinator) Research Workers investigate in parallel Up to 5 Synthesize Merge findings into a plan 1 (coordinator) Implement Execute the plan 1–3 Verify Fresh worker validates results 1

docs.title: Worker Roles
docs.text:
WORKERS = {
    "architect": ArchitectAgent,
    "coder": ArchitectAgent,
    "devops": ArchitectAgent,
    "analyst": DataAnalyst,
    "researcher": ResearchWorker,
    "verifier": VerificationWorker,
}

docs.title: Scratchpad
docs.text:
Workers communicate via a shared scratchpad — a filesystem directory containing intermediate artifacts:

workspace/coordinator_sessions/{session_id}/
├── plan.json           # Decomposed task plan
├── research_1.md       # Worker 1 findings
├── research_2.md       # Worker 2 findings
├── synthesis.md        # Merged findings
├── implementation.md   # Final output
└── verification.md     # Verification report

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Setting Value Description Max workers 5 Parallel research workers Timeout per phase 120s Phase execution limit Max total time 600s Total coordination limit

docs.title: Example Flow
docs.text:
User: "Compare Python web frameworks for a REST API and build a prototype"

  1. Decompose: Split into "research frameworks" + "build prototype"
  2. Research: 3 workers investigate Flask, FastAPI, Django REST
  3. Synthesize: Merge into comparison table with recommendation
  4. Implement: Coder builds FastAPI prototype
  5. Verify: Verifier checks code runs and matches requirements

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Architecture: Agent System — routing to coordinator
  • User Guide: Research Mode — user-facing guide

docs.title: Dispatcher
docs.text: Event bus and async task queue management.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose agents/dispatcher.py Dispatcher class — queue routing and concurrency

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The Dispatcher manages async task execution for long-running operations that shouldn't block the chat response:

  • Image generation (10–60 seconds)
  • 3D model rendering (30–120 seconds)
  • Batch operations

docs.title: Queue Architecture
docs.text:
graph LR
    API[API Request] --> D[Dispatcher]
    D --> Q1["queue:default · 5 workers"]
    D --> Q2["queue:image · 2 workers"]
    D --> Q3["queue:3d · 1 worker"]
    D --> Q4["queue:action_figure · 1 worker"]

docs.title: Queue Configuration
docs.text:
Queue Workers Purpose default 5 Chat, code, general tasks image 2 ComfyUI image generation 3d 1 3D mesh reconstruction action_figure 1 Articulated figure design

Worker counts are limited to prevent GPU memory exhaustion.

docs.title: Task Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. API receives request, router classifies intent
  2. If async (IMAGE, 3D): Dispatcher queues the task, returns task ID immediately
  3. Worker picks up task from queue
  4. Worker executes (calls ComfyUI, 3D pipeline, etc.)
  5. Result is stored; task status updated to completed
  6. Client polls GET /v1/tasks/{task_id} for result

docs.title: Redis Backend
docs.text:
The Dispatcher uses Redis for queue persistence:

  • Tasks survive container restarts
  • Workers dequeue atomically
  • Failed tasks can be retried

docs.title: Governance
docs.text: Request approval workflow for privileged operations.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose agents/governance.py Governance engine — state machine, assessment, persistence

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: When an agent attempts a privileged or risky operation (dangerous commands, system changes), the Governance module intercepts and routes the request through an approval workflow.

docs.title: State Machine
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PendingReview: Submit request
    PendingReview --> Approved: Admin approves
    PendingReview --> Denied: Admin denies
    PendingReview --> AutoApproved: Policy auto-approve
    PendingReview --> Expired: Timeout (24h)
    Approved --> [*]: Execute action
    Denied --> [*]
    AutoApproved --> [*]: Execute action
    Expired --> [*]

docs.title: Request Types
docs.text: Type Triggers PACKAGE Agent or user requests a pip install or apt install MODEL Agent requests access to a restricted or new model PERMISSION Escalating file/tool access levels FEATURE Enabling a new system feature GROUNDING_WEB User requests internet/web search grounding access GROUNDING_DOCS User requests knowledge-base document grounding access OTHER Any miscellaneous privileged request privilege_escalation Requesting higher security level

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text:
The governance engine performs a risk assessment:

risk = assess_risk(action, command, context)
# Returns: LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, CRITICAL
Risk Action LOW Auto-approve MEDIUM Notify admin, execute HIGH Require admin approval CRITICAL Block, notify admin

docs.title: Storage
docs.text: Governance state is persisted in workflow_state/governance_requests.json.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Architecture: Security Model — security context
  • User Guide: Governance — user-facing guide
  • Developer: Governance API — API reference

docs.title: GPU Allocator
docs.text: VRAM reservation and model scheduling.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose agents/gpu_allocator.py GPU memory management

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The GPU Allocator tracks VRAM usage and prevents out-of-memory errors by:

  • Tracking which models are loaded in VRAM
  • Preventing concurrent operations that exceed VRAM budget
  • Coordinating between Ollama, ComfyUI, and Voice Engine

docs.title: Execution Node (RTX 5060 Ti — 16 GB)
docs.text:
Consumer VRAM Priority qwen3.6:27b ~6 GB High nemotron-orchestrator:8b ~5 GB High llama-guard-3:8b ~5 GB Medium ComfyUI (FLUX) ~10 GB On-demand Voice (Qwen3-TTS) ~3 GB On-demand

Not All Fit Simultaneously

Total VRAM needed for all models exceeds 16 GB. The allocator and Ollama cooperate to load/unload models dynamically.

docs.title: Interaction with Ollama
docs.text:
Ollama handles its own model lifecycle:

Ollama Setting Effect OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=3 Maximum 3 models in VRAM OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=10m Unload idle models after 10 min

The GPU Allocator complements Ollama by scheduling non-Ollama GPU tasks (ComfyUI, voice synthesis) to avoid conflicts.

docs.text:

  • Admin: Scaling — adding GPU capacity

from agents.logger_setup import setup_logging

docs.title: Log Levels
docs.text:
| Level | Use |

|-------|-----|

| DEBUG | Detailed diagnostic info |

| Docker logs | JSON | Picked up by Promtail ? knuth |

docs.title: Contextual Logging
docs.text:
Always include contextual information:

logger.info(f"Intent classified: {intent} conf={confidence} session={session_id}")

logger.error(f"Ollama request failed: {error} model={model} session={session_id}")

docs.title: MarsRL Loop
docs.text: Inference-time quality verification: Solver → Verifier → Corrector.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose agents/mars_loop.py MarsRLLoop class — orchestration agents/architect_agent.py Solver (response generation) agents/verifier_agent.py Verifier (3-layer validation) agents/corrector_agent.py Corrector (error rewriting)

docs.title: run() Method
docs.text:
async def run(
    messages: list[dict],
    intent: str,
    session_id: str,
    token: Optional[str] = None,
    stream_callback: Optional[Callable] = None,
) -> MarsLoopResult

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Solver generates response
2. Verifier runs 3 checks:
   a. AST parse (code only)
   b. Coherence check
   c. Safety check (llama-guard-3)
3. If score ≥ 0.60: PASS → return
4. If score < 0.60 and iterations < 2:
   a. Corrector rewrites response
   b. Go to step 2
5. If iterations ≥ 2: return best attempt

docs.title: MarsLoopResult
docs.text:
@dataclass
class MarsLoopResult:
    response: str
    iterations: int
    solver_score: float
    corrector_invoked: bool
    final_score: float
    trace_id: Optional[str]
    token: Optional[str]
    template_metadata: dict
    metadata: dict

docs.title: Layer 1: AST Parse
docs.text:
try:
    ast.parse(code_content)
    # Score unchanged
except SyntaxError:
    score -= 0.40

Only runs when the response contains Python code blocks.

docs.title: Layer 2: Coherence
docs.text:
Checks for:

  • Non-empty response
  • No repetition loops (same phrase repeated 3+ times)
  • Response addresses the user's question

Score penalty: −0.25 per failed check.

docs.title: Layer 3: Safety
docs.text: Uses llama-guard-3:8b to check for harmful content. A safety failure forces score to 0.0 and blocks the response.

docs.title: Langfuse Integration
docs.text:
Every run creates:

  • A trace with session ID and intent metadata
  • Spans for each step (solver, verifier, corrector)
  • Scores recorded as float 0.0–1.0

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Architecture: MarsRL — design document
  • Architecture: ADR-004 — decision rationale

docs.title: Memory
docs.text: Agent-side memory implementation. Covers the local, in-process subsystems (Skills Memory, Preferences, Session Context) plus the HTTP integration into the networked MemPalace service.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose agents/memory_system.py Skills Memory — rule-based knowledge agents/preferences.py Per-user preferences agents/context_manager.py Session context lifecycle agents/main.py (_mempalace_extract_http) HTTP caller for /v1/extract agents/mempalace_client.py Legacy embedded-library client (one residual caller; deprecation candidate)

docs.title: Skills Memory
docs.text: File-backed JSON store at agents/skills_memory.json.

docs.title: Domains
docs.text: Domain Examples visual_rules Art style hints — "cyberpunk: neon lighting" coding_rules Code conventions — "use type hints" general_rules Communication style — "be concise" session_summaries Auto-generated conversation recaps

docs.title: API
docs.text:
memory = SkillsMemory()
memory.add_rule("coding_rules", "python", "Always use f-strings for formatting")
rules = memory.get_relevant_rules("Write a Python function", "coding_rules")
summaries = memory.get_recent_summaries(n=5, owner_id="user_001")

docs.title: Session Context
docs.text:
Active conversation state persisted in agents/context_sessions/:

context = ContextManager()
session = context.get_session(session_id)
session.add_message({"role": "user", "content": "Hello"})
history = session.get_messages()

docs.title: Preferences
docs.text:
Per-user configuration:

prefs = Preferences()
prefs.set("user_001", "response_style", "concise")
prefs.set("user_001", "default_model", "qwen3.6:27b")
style = prefs.get("user_001", "response_style")  # "concise"

docs.title: TRAIN intent → MemPalace integration
docs.text:
When a user says "Remember that I prefer bullet points", the router classifies it as TRAIN and writes to two places:

  1. Skills Memory as a rule: 
    memory.add_rule("general_rules", "format", "Use bullet points in responses")
  2. MemPalace as a procedural memory (semantic recall in future sessions): 
    mempalace_client.store(
    content="format: Use bullet points in responses",
    memory_type="procedural",
    domain="general",
    owner_id=owner_id,
)

The MemPalace write currently goes through agents/mempalace_client.py in a try/except: pass fallback — it's a legacy embedded-library client that survives only for this one caller. The dominant integration is the HTTP call to MEMPALACE_API_URL (http://192.168.2.102:8200) used by agents/main.py:_mempalace_extract_http after each turn.

docs.title: Per-turn extraction
docs.text:
After every model response completes, agents/main.py POSTs a short conversation summary to MEMPALACE_API_URL/v1/extract:

async def _mempalace_extract_http(conversation: str, owner_id: str | None) -> int:
    """POST conversation text to the MemPalace /v1/extract endpoint."""
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30) as client:
        resp = await client.post(
            f"{_MEMPALACE_API_URL}/v1/extract",
            json={"conversation": conversation, "owner_id": owner_id},
        )
        return len(resp.json())

MemPalace runs the conversation through an LLM extractor, embeds the resulting facts, and persists everything in a single transaction alongside an extraction-attempt audit row. See MemPalace Deep Dive — Extraction Pipeline.

To add a new metric:

from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, Gauge

my_counter = Counter(

scrape_configs:

    static_configs:
      - targets: ['192.168.2.101:8008']
