docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Symptom-based guides for diagnosing and resolving issues. Each page covers a specific component.

Component Common Issues Ollama Model load failures, OOM, slow inference ComfyUI Workflow errors, VRAM conflicts SPIRE Attestation failures, expired SVIDs Network Connectivity, DNS, Traefik routing GPU Driver issues, VRAM, thermal throttling Agent Runtime Crashes, timeouts, intent routing Langfuse Tracing failures, database connection Docker Container failures, disk space, networking Voice STT/TTS failures, audio issues

Tip

For quick answers, try the Troubleshooting FAQ first.

docs.title: Runtime Won't Start
docs.text:
Symptom: Container exits immediately or fails health check.

Diagnose:

docker logs agent-runtime --tail 50

Common causes:

  • Missing environment variables in network.env
  • Port conflict on 8000
  • Python import errors (missing dependencies)

Fix:

  • Verify network.env has all required variables
  • Rebuild the image: docker compose build agent-runtime
  • Check for syntax errors in agent code

docs.title: Intent Misclassification
docs.text:
Symptom: Messages routed to wrong agent (e.g., "draw a cat" goes to general chat).

Diagnose:

Check Langfuse traces for the intent classification scores.

Fix:

  1. Add better examples to agents/intent_capabilities.py
  2. Try a larger router model
  3. Adjust confidence thresholds in the Router

docs.title: Response Timeout
docs.text:
Symptom: Chat hangs or returns timeout error.

Diagnose:

# Check if Ollama is responsive
curl http://192.168.2.101:11434/api/tags

# Check agent runtime logs
docker logs agent-runtime --tail 20

Fix:

  1. Increase STREAM_TIMEOUT in agent configuration
  2. Check Ollama health and restart if needed
  3. A very large context may cause slow responses — reduce history

docs.title: MarsRL Verification Loop
docs.text:
Symptom: Response takes very long, logs show multiple "Verifier: FAIL" entries.

Fix:

  1. Reduce max_iter in MarsRL config (try 1 instead of 2)
  2. Lower pass_threshold (try 0.50 instead of 0.70)
  3. Check if the verifier model is the right size for the task

docs.title: Memory/Context Issues
docs.text:
Symptom: Agent doesn't remember previous messages in conversation.

Check:

  • Session ID is being passed correctly
  • Context window isn't exceeded
  • Memory system (PostgreSQL + pgvector) is healthy

docs.title: Workflow Execution Fails
docs.text:
Symptom: Image generation returns an error.

Diagnose:

# Check ComfyUI health
curl http://192.168.2.101:8188/system_stats

# Check logs
docker logs comfyui --tail 50

Fix:

  • Verify the workflow JSON is valid
  • Check that all required custom nodes are installed
  • Restart ComfyUI: docker compose restart comfyui

docs.title: VRAM Conflict with Ollama
docs.text:
Symptom: ComfyUI fails with OOM when Ollama models are loaded.

Fix:

  1. Set OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=5m for faster model unloading
  2. Wait for Ollama models to unload before generating images
  3. Reduce OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=1 to free VRAM faster

docs.title: Missing Custom Nodes
docs.text:
Symptom: Workflow references nodes that don't exist.

Diagnose:

Check the ComfyUI log for "Node type not found" errors.

Fix:

# Install missing nodes
docker exec comfyui pip install <package>
# Or clone into custom_nodes/
docker exec comfyui git clone <repo> /comfyui/custom_nodes/<name>
docker compose restart comfyui

docs.title: Slow Image Generation
docs.text:
Symptom: Images take much longer than expected.

Check:

  1. GPU utilization during generation: nvidia-smi
  2. Image resolution — larger = slower
  3. Number of sampling steps — more = slower
  4. Is Ollama competing for VRAM?

docs.title: ComfyUI Web Interface Not Loading
docs.text:
Symptom: http://192.168.2.101:8188 doesn't respond.

Fix:

docker compose ps comfyui  # Check status
docker compose restart comfyui
docker logs comfyui --tail 20

docs.title: Container Won't Start
docs.text:
Symptom: Container exits immediately after starting.

Diagnose:

docker logs <container-name> --tail 50

docker inspect <container-name> --format='{{.State.ExitCode}}'

Common causes:

  • Missing environment variables
  • Port already in use
  • Volume mount permission issues
  • Image not built

docs.title: Disk Space Full
docs.text:
Symptom: Containers fail to start or operations fail with I/O errors.

Diagnose:

df -h
docker system df

Fix:

# Remove unused images, containers, volumes
docker system prune -a --volumes

# Remove old build caches
docker builder prune

Warning

docker system prune --volumes removes unused volumes. Make sure your data is backed up first.

docs.title: Network Connectivity Between Containers
docs.text:
Symptom: One container can't reach another.

Diagnose:

# Check networks
docker network ls
docker network inspect <network-name>

# Test from inside a container
docker exec <container> ping <other-container>

Fix:

  • Ensure both containers are on the same Docker network
  • Check Docker Compose networks: configuration
  • Use service names (not container names) for inter-container DNS

docs.title: Permission Denied on Volumes
docs.text:
Symptom: Container logs show permission errors on mounted volumes.

Fix:

# Fix ownership
sudo chown -R 1000:1000 /path/to/volume

# Or use user mapping in docker-compose
user: "1000:1000"

docs.title: Docker Compose Version Issues
docs.text:
Symptom: docker compose commands fail with syntax errors.

Fix:

  • Use docker compose (v2, built-in) not docker-compose (v1, standalone)
  • Verify version: docker compose version
  • Update Docker Engine for the latest Compose

docs.title: GPU Not Detected
docs.text:
Symptom: nvidia-smi fails or shows no GPU.

Fix:

  1. Install/update NVIDIA driver: 
    sudo apt install nvidia-driver-550
sudo reboot
  2. Verify: nvidia-smi

docs.title: GPU Not Available in Docker
docs.text:
Symptom: nvidia-smi works on host but not in containers.

Fix:

# Install NVIDIA Container Toolkit
curl -fsSL https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/gpgkey | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg
curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/stable/deb/nvidia-container-toolkit.list | \
    sed 's#deb https://#deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg] https://#g' | \
    sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list
sudo apt update
sudo apt install nvidia-container-toolkit
sudo nvidia-ctk runtime configure --runtime=docker
sudo systemctl restart docker

Test: docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-base-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi

docs.title: Thermal Throttling
docs.text:
Symptom: Performance degrades after sustained workloads.

Diagnose:

nvidia-smi -q -d TEMPERATURE

Fix:

  • Improve case airflow
  • Set fan curve to aggressive
  • Add thermal pads/paste if needed
  • Reduce sustained workload intensity

docs.title: Multi-GPU Selection
docs.text:
Symptom: Wrong GPU is being used.

Fix:

Set CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES in docker-compose:

environment:
  - CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0  # Or 1, 2, etc.

docs.title: Driver/CUDA Version Mismatch
docs.text:
Symptom: CUDA errors or library version conflicts.

Check:

nvidia-smi  # Shows driver and max CUDA version
nvcc --version  # Shows installed CUDA toolkit version

Ensure the Docker images use a compatible CUDA version.

docs.title: Langfuse Dashboard Not Loading
docs.text:
Symptom: http://192.168.2.102:3000 doesn't respond.

Diagnose:

docker compose ps langfuse
docker logs langfuse --tail 30

Fix:

  • Check PostgreSQL is running (Langfuse depends on it)
  • Verify LANGFUSE_DATABASE_URL in environment
  • Restart: docker compose restart langfuse

docs.title: Traces Not Appearing
docs.text:
Symptom: Conversations happen but no traces in Langfuse.

Diagnose:

Check agent runtime logs for Langfuse connection errors.

Fix:

  1. Verify LANGFUSE_HOST, LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY, LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY in network.env
  2. Test connectivity from agent runtime: curl http://192.168.2.102:3000/api/public/health
  3. Check that the Langfuse SDK is initialized in the agent code

docs.title: Database Connection Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: Langfuse logs show PostgreSQL connection refused.

Fix:

  1. Verify PostgreSQL is running: docker compose ps postgres
  2. Check credentials in Langfuse environment variables
  3. Verify the database exists: 
    docker compose exec postgres psql -U postgres -l

docs.title: High Disk Usage
docs.text:
Symptom: PostgreSQL database growing very large from traces.

Fix:

  1. Set a retention policy in Langfuse settings
  2. Archive old traces
  3. Vacuum the database: 
    docker compose exec postgres psql -U postgres -c "VACUUM FULL;"

docs.title: Traefik Not Routing
docs.text:
Symptom: Requests to 192.168.2.103 return 404 or timeout.

Diagnose:

# Check Traefik dashboard
curl http://192.168.2.103:8080/api/overview

# Check active routers
curl http://192.168.2.103:8080/api/http/routers | python -m json.tool

Fix:

  1. Verify Docker labels on the target service
  2. Check that the service is on the correct Docker network
  3. Restart Traefik: docker compose restart traefik

docs.title: Service Unreachable Between Nodes
docs.text:
Symptom: One node can't reach a service on another node.

Diagnose:

# Basic connectivity
ping 192.168.2.101

# Port check
curl -v http://192.168.2.101:11434/api/tags

Fix:

  1. Check firewall rules: sudo ufw status or sudo iptables -L
  2. Verify both nodes are on the same subnet
  3. Check Docker network configuration

docs.title: DNS Resolution Failures
docs.text:
Symptom: Services can't resolve hostnames.

Fix:

  • Use IP addresses instead of hostnames in network.env
  • Check Docker's DNS: docker exec <container> cat /etc/resolv.conf
  • Add static entries if needed: extra_hosts in docker-compose

docs.title: Tailscale VPN Issues
docs.text:
Symptom: Remote access via Tailscale doesn't work.

Fix:

  1. Check Tailscale status: tailscale status
  2. Verify the node is online in Tailscale admin
  3. Check that services listen on 0.0.0.0 (not 127.0.0.1)
  4. Verify Tailscale IP routing

docs.title: Port Conflicts
docs.text:
Symptom: Container fails to start with "port already in use".

Diagnose:

sudo ss -tlnp | grep <port>

Fix:

  • Stop the conflicting process
  • Or remap the port in docker-compose.yml
  • See Port Map for all used ports

docs.title: Model Fails to Load
docs.text:
Symptom: Error like model not found or failed to load model.

Diagnose:

# List available models
docker exec ollama ollama list

# Check Ollama logs
docker logs ollama --tail 50

Fix:

  • Pull the model: docker exec ollama ollama pull <model>
  • Check disk space: docker exec ollama df -h /root/.ollama

docs.title: Out of Memory (OOM)
docs.text:
Symptom: CUDA out of memory or model loading stalls.

Diagnose:

docker exec ollama nvidia-smi

Fix:

  1. Reduce loaded models: OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=2
  2. Use smaller quantizations (q4_K_M instead of q8_0)
  3. Reduce context window: OLLAMA_NUM_CTX=4096
  4. Restart Ollama: docker compose restart ollama

docs.title: Slow Inference
docs.text:
Symptom: Tokens per second much lower than expected.

Diagnose:

# Check GPU utilization
docker exec ollama nvidia-smi -l 1

# Check if Flash Attention is enabled
docker inspect ollama | grep FLASH

Fix:

  1. Enable Flash Attention: OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1
  2. Reduce OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL if VRAM is constrained
  3. Check for thermal throttling: nvidia-smi -q -d TEMPERATURE
  4. Ensure no other GPU processes are running

docs.title: Ollama Won't Start
docs.text:
Symptom: Container exits immediately.

Diagnose:

docker logs ollama

Fix:

  • Check NVIDIA Container Toolkit: docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-base-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi
  • Verify GPU runtime: ensure runtime: nvidia is in docker-compose
  • Check port conflicts: ensure port 11434 is free

docs.title: Model Corruption
docs.text:
Symptom: Model loads but produces garbage output.

Fix:

docker exec ollama ollama rm <model>
docker exec ollama ollama pull <model>

docs.title: Agent Attestation Fails
docs.text:
Symptom: SPIRE agent can't connect to server. Log shows attestation errors.

Diagnose:

docker compose exec spire-agent /opt/spire/bin/spire-agent healthcheck
docker logs spire-agent --tail 30

Fix:

  1. Generate a fresh join token on the Control Node: 
    docker compose exec spire-server /opt/spire/bin/spire-server token generate \
    -spiffeID spiffe://home-ai-lab/execution-node -ttl 3600
  2. Update the agent configuration with the new token
  3. Restart the agent: docker compose restart spire-agent

docs.title: Expired SVIDs
docs.text:
Symptom: Service-to-service calls fail with TLS certificate errors.

Diagnose:

docker compose exec spire-agent /opt/spire/bin/spire-agent api fetch x509

Check the expiry timestamp.

Fix:

  • SVIDs auto-rotate. If they've stopped rotating, the agent may have lost connection to the server
  • Re-attest the agent with a new join token
  • Verify SPIRE server is healthy: docker compose exec spire-server /opt/spire/bin/spire-server healthcheck

docs.title: SPIRE Server Unreachable
docs.text:
Symptom: Agent logs show "connection refused" to server.

Fix:

  1. Verify the server is running: docker compose ps spire-server
  2. Check network connectivity: ping 192.168.2.102
  3. Verify the port (8081) is accessible
  4. Check firewall rules

docs.title: Registration Entry Issues
docs.text:
Symptom: Agent is attested but SVIDs don't have the right SPIFFE IDs.

Check:

docker compose exec spire-server /opt/spire/bin/spire-server entry show

Verify entries exist for each service with the correct selectors.

docs.title: Speech-to-Text (STT) Not Working
docs.text:
Symptom: Microphone records but no transcription appears.

Diagnose:

# Check Whisper service
curl http://192.168.2.101:9000/health
docker logs whisper --tail 30

Fix:

  1. Restart Whisper: docker compose restart whisper
  2. Check that the Whisper model is loaded
  3. Verify audio format compatibility (16kHz WAV preferred)

docs.title: Text-to-Speech (TTS) Not Working
docs.text:
Symptom: No audio plays after responses.

Diagnose:

# Check Piper service
curl http://192.168.2.101:5500/health
docker logs piper --tail 30

Fix:

  1. Check browser audio permissions
  2. Verify Piper voice model is installed
  3. Restart Piper: docker compose restart piper

docs.title: Poor Transcription Quality
docs.text:
Symptom: Whisper transcribes words incorrectly.

Fix:

  1. Reduce background noise
  2. Speak clearly and at normal pace
  3. Use a better microphone
  4. Try a larger Whisper model (e.g., medium instead of base)

docs.title: Audio Latency
docs.text:
Symptom: Long delay between speaking and transcription.

Fix:

  1. Use a smaller Whisper model for faster processing
  2. Check CPU/GPU load during transcription
  3. Reduce audio chunk size for faster streaming

docs.title: Browser Microphone Issues
docs.text:
Symptom: "Microphone not found" or permission denied.

Fix:

  1. Check browser settings → Site Settings → Microphone
  2. Ensure the page is served over HTTPS (or localhost)
  3. Try a different browser
  4. Check OS audio input settings

docs.title: Tutorials
docs.text:
Hands-on, narrative walkthroughs that guide you through complete tasks from start to finish.

Prerequisites

All tutorials assume you have a running Memex deployment. If not, start with the Getting Started guide.

docs.title: Beginner
docs.text: Tutorial What You'll Learn Your First Chat Send a message, understand the response flow Generate an Image Create AI art with text prompts

docs.title: Intermediate
docs.text: Tutorial What You'll Learn Build a Voice Assistant Set up voice input/output Create IoT Automation Control smart home devices via chat Research a Topic Use Research Mode for deep-dive analysis Train Model Preferences Teach the system your style

docs.title: Advanced
docs.text: Tutorial What You'll Learn Build a 3D Model Generate 3D assets from text Multi-Agent Workflow Orchestrate agents for complex tasks Create a Custom Agent Build and register your own agent

docs.title: Build a 3D Model
docs.text: Generate 3D assets from text descriptions.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to trigger 3D generation
  • The multi-stage 3D pipeline
  • How to iterate on results

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• ComfyUI running with 3D-capable models
  • Sufficient GPU VRAM (16 GB+ recommended)

docs.title: Step 1: Describe Your Object
docs.text:
In the Hive UI:

Generate a 3D model of a medieval sword

The Router classifies this as 3d_generation and routes to the 3D Pipeline agent.

docs.title: Step 2: Understand the Pipeline
docs.text:
The 3D generation pipeline follows multiple stages:

  1. Prompt Enhancement — the solver enriches your description
  2. Reference Image — ComfyUI generates a reference image
  3. Multi-View Generation — creates views from multiple angles
  4. Mesh Reconstruction — builds 3D geometry from the views
  5. Texture Mapping — applies textures to the mesh

docs.title: Step 3: Review the Output
docs.text:
The agent returns:

  • A preview render of the 3D model
  • Download link for the mesh file (.obj or .glb)
  • The enhanced prompt used

docs.title: Step 4: Iterate
docs.text:
Refine your model:

Make the sword blade more curved and add runes on the blade

The pipeline re-runs with the adjusted description.

docs.title: Step 5: Action Figure Mode
docs.text:
For character models, use the Action Figure pipeline:

Create an action figure of a robot samurai

This uses a specialized workflow optimized for character models with proper proportions and articulation.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Start simple: basic shapes before complex scenes
  • Specify material: "metallic", "wooden", "glass"
  • Specify style: "realistic", "low-poly", "cartoon"
  • Single objects work better than full scenes

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• User Guide: 3D Generation — full reference
  • Modules: 3D Pipeline — technical details

docs.title: Create a Custom Agent
docs.text: Build, register, and deploy your own specialized agent.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• The agent interface and registration API
  • How to implement custom logic
  • How to integrate with existing tools

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Development environment set up (see Local Development)
  • Understanding of the agent architecture (see Agent System)

docs.title: Step 1: Create the Agent File
docs.text:
Create agents/my_agent.py:

from agents.lamport import Coordinator
from agents.logger_setup import get_logger

logger = get_logger("my_agent")


class MyAgent:
    """A custom agent that handles a specific domain."""

    def __init__(self):
        self.name = "my_agent"
        self.description = "Handles custom-domain tasks"

    async def handle(self, message: str, context: dict) -> str:
        """Process a user message and return a response."""
        logger.info(f"MyAgent handling: {message[:50]}...")

        # Your custom logic here
        # Use context for session info, owner_id, etc.

        result = await self._process(message, context)
        return result

    async def _process(self, message: str, context: dict) -> str:
        # Implement your logic
        return f"MyAgent processed: {message}"

docs.title: Step 2: Register the Intent
docs.text:
Add your agent's intent to the Router's capability map in agents/intent_capabilities.py:

INTENT_MAP = {
    # ... existing intents ...
    "my_domain": {
        "agent": "my_agent",
        "description": "Handles custom-domain tasks",
        "examples": [
            "do my custom thing",
            "handle this special request",
        ],
    },
}

docs.title: Step 3: Register with the Coordinator
docs.text:
In agents/coordinator.py, register your agent:

from agents.my_agent import MyAgent

# In the initialization
self.agents["my_agent"] = MyAgent()

docs.title: Step 4: Add Tools (Optional)
docs.text:
If your agent needs tools, create them in core_tools/:

# core_tools/my_tool.py
class MyTool:
    name = "my_tool"
    description = "Does a specific thing"

    async def execute(self, params: dict) -> dict:
        # Tool logic
        return {"result": "done"}

docs.title: Step 5: Test
docs.text:
# Send a test message
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "do my custom thing"}]
    }'

Verify your agent handles the request by checking logs and Langfuse traces.

docs.title: Step 6: Deploy
docs.text:
Rebuild and restart the agent runtime:

cd execution_plane
docker compose build agent-runtime
docker compose up -d agent-runtime

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Developer Guide: Adding Agents — detailed guide
  • Developer Guide: Adding Tools — tool development

docs.title: Your First Chat
docs.text: Send your first message to Memex and understand how it processes your request.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to access the chat interface

  • How messages flow through the system

  • How to interpret responses

docs.title: Step 1: Open the Chat Interface
docs.text:
Navigate to the Hive UI:

http://192.168.2.103/

You'll see the chat interface with a text input at the bottom.

docs.title: Step 2: Send a Message
docs.text:
Type a simple question:

What can you help me with?

Press Enter or click the send button.

docs.title: Step 3: Watch the Flow
docs.text:
Behind the scenes, your message flows through:

  1. Hive UI ? sends to Agent Runtime API

  2. Router (nemotron-orchestrator:8b) ? classifies your intent as general_chat

  3. Coordinator ? dispatches to the solver

  4. Solver (qwen3.6:27b) ? generates a response

  5. MarsRL Verifier (llama-guard-3:8b) ? checks safety and quality

  6. Response streams back to the UI

You should see a streaming response appear in real-time.

docs.title: Step 4: Try Different Intents
docs.text:
Each of these triggers a different agent:

| Message | Intent | Agent |

|---------|--------|-------|

| "What's 2+2?" | general_chat | Solver |

| "Draw a sunset" | image_generation | Image Agent |

| "Turn on the lights" | iot_control | IoT Agent |

| "Generate a 3D model of a chair" | 3d_generation | 3D Pipeline |

docs.title: Step 5: Check the Trace
docs.text:
Open Langfuse at http://192.168.2.102:3000 to see the full trace of your conversation, including:

  • Intent classification scores

  • Model latency

  • Token usage

  • Verifier results

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Generate an Image  try creative generation

  • User Guide: Chat  full chat feature reference

docs.title: Generate an Image
docs.text: Create AI-generated images using natural language prompts.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to trigger the Image Agent
  • How prompts are enhanced
  • How to adjust style and quality

docs.title: Step 1: Open the Chat
docs.text: Navigate to http://192.168.2.103/ and open a new conversation.

docs.title: Step 2: Describe Your Image
docs.text:
Type a natural language description:

Create a watercolor painting of a cozy mountain cabin at sunset

The Router detects image_generation intent and routes to the Image Agent.

docs.title: Step 3: Understand the Pipeline
docs.text:
The Image Agent:

  1. Enhances your prompt using the solver model to add artistic details
  2. Selects a ComfyUI workflow (e.g., workflow_hunyuan_paint.json)
  3. Queues the job on ComfyUI at 192.168.2.101:8188
  4. Returns the image in the chat

docs.title: Step 4: Iterate
docs.text:
Refine your image with follow-up messages:

Make it more vibrant with autumn colors

The agent maintains context and adjusts the prompt.

docs.title: Step 5: Advanced Prompting
docs.text:
For more control, specify:

  • Style: "in the style of Studio Ghibli"
  • Medium: "oil painting", "digital art", "photograph"
  • Composition: "close-up", "wide angle", "bird's eye view"
  • Lighting: "golden hour", "dramatic lighting", "soft ambient"

docs.title: Example Gallery
docs.text: Prompt Description "A cyberpunk city at night, neon lights, rain" Sci-fi cityscape "Portrait of a cat wearing a crown, oil painting" Stylized pet portrait "Minimalist logo design for a coffee shop" Design work

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• User Guide: Art Studio — full Art Studio reference
  • Build a 3D Model — go from 2D to 3D

docs.title: Create IoT Automation
docs.text: Control smart home devices through natural language chat commands.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How Memex connects to Home Assistant
  • How to issue device commands via chat
  • How to create automation routines

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Home Assistant running and accessible
  • Devices configured in Home Assistant
  • HA_URL and HA_TOKEN set in network.env

docs.title: Step 1: Verify Connection
docs.text:
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $HA_TOKEN" \
    http://<ha-ip>:8123/api/states | python -m json.tool | head -20

You should see a list of entity states.

docs.title: Step 2: Simple Commands
docs.text:
In the Hive UI, try:

Turn on the living room lights

The Router classifies this as iot_control and routes to the IoT Agent, which calls the Home Assistant API.

docs.title: Step 3: Query Device State
docs.text:
What's the temperature in the bedroom?

The IoT Agent queries the relevant sensor entity and responds with the current value.

docs.title: Step 4: Complex Commands
docs.text:
Set the living room lights to 50% brightness and warm white

The agent translates this into the appropriate Home Assistant service call with brightness and color temperature parameters.

docs.title: Step 5: Automations via Chat
docs.text:
Every weekday at 7am, turn on the kitchen lights and set the thermostat to 72

This creates a Home Assistant automation through the API.

docs.title: Example Commands
docs.text: Command What Happens "Turn off all lights" Calls light.turn_off for all light entities "Lock the front door" Calls lock.lock for the door lock entity "Is the garage door open?" Queries the garage door cover entity state "Set thermostat to 68" Calls climate.set_temperature

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• User Guide: IoT Control — full IoT reference
  • Modules: Home Assistant Tool — technical details

docs.title: Multi-Agent Workflow
docs.text: Orchestrate multiple agents to handle complex, multi-step tasks.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How agents collaborate on complex requests
  • How the Coordinator manages agent handoffs
  • How to craft prompts that trigger multi-agent flows

docs.title: Step 1: Understand Agent Collaboration
docs.text:
When a task requires multiple capabilities, the Coordinator orchestrates:

sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant C as Coordinator
    participant S as Solver
    participant I as Image Agent
    participant F as File Ops

    U->>C: "Write a blog post about AI and include an illustration"
    C->>S: Generate blog post text
    S-->>C: Blog post content
    C->>I: Generate illustration
    I-->>C: Image
    C->>F: Save both to workspace
    F-->>C: Done
    C-->>U: Blog post + image saved

docs.title: Step 2: Try a Multi-Step Request
docs.text:
Research the history of neural networks, write a summary, and create an infographic showing the timeline

This triggers:

  1. Search Tool — gathers research material
  2. Solver — writes the summary
  3. Image Agent — creates the timeline infographic

docs.title: Step 3: Code + Documentation
docs.text:
Write a Python function to calculate Fibonacci numbers, add docstrings, and create a test file

This triggers:

  1. Solver — writes the function
  2. Solver — adds documentation
  3. File Ops — saves the code
  4. Solver — writes tests
  5. File Ops — saves the test file

docs.title: Step 4: IoT + Voice
docs.text:
When someone says "movie time", dim the living room lights to 20% and turn on the TV

This combines:

  1. IoT Agent — creates the automation
  2. Voice understanding — sets up the trigger phrase

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Be explicit about what you want: list each step
  • Use "and" or numbered lists to signal multi-step tasks
  • Complex workflows may take longer — watch the streaming output

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Architecture: Agent System — how agents interact
  • Modules: Coordinator — orchestration details

docs.title: Research a Topic
docs.text: Use Research Mode for deep-dive analysis with web search and multi-step reasoning.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to activate Research Mode
  • How the system gathers and synthesizes information
  • How to refine research results

docs.title: Step 1: Activate Research Mode
docs.text:
In the Hive UI, either:

  • Toggle the Research Mode switch, or
  • Prefix your message with a research-style query

docs.title: Step 2: Ask a Research Question
docs.text:
Research the latest developments in quantum computing error correction

The system will:

  1. Break down your question into sub-queries
  2. Search the web using the Search Tool
  3. Synthesize findings from multiple sources
  4. Cite sources in the response

docs.title: Step 3: Review Results
docs.text:
The response includes:

  • A structured summary
  • Key findings organized by theme
  • Source citations with links
  • Areas of uncertainty or debate

docs.title: Step 4: Go Deeper
docs.text:
Follow up with targeted questions:

Tell me more about surface codes vs. color codes

The agent maintains context from the initial research and provides more detailed analysis.

docs.title: Step 5: Export
docs.text:
Summarize your findings as a markdown report

The agent generates a structured document you can copy or save.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Be specific: "quantum computing error correction in 2024" vs. "quantum computing"
  • Ask for comparisons: "Compare X vs Y"
  • Request specific formats: "Give me a table comparing approaches"

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• User Guide: Research Mode — full reference
  • Modules: Search Tool — how search works

docs.title: Train Model Preferences
docs.text: Teach Memex your preferences using GRPO training.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to provide preference feedback

  • How feedback is collected and stored

  • How GRPO training improves responses

docs.title: Step 1: Interact Naturally
docs.text: Use the system normally. Over time, you'll notice responses you like and responses you'd change.

docs.title: Step 2: Provide Explicit Feedback
docs.text:
When you see a response you like or dislike:

  • Thumbs up  marks the response as preferred

  • Thumbs down  marks it as rejected

  • Edit and resubmit  teaches the correct response

docs.title: Step 3: Teach Preferences via Chat
docs.text:
You can also state preferences directly:

Remember that I prefer concise answers with code examples

I like responses formatted as bullet points

These are stored in Skills Memory under your owner_id.

docs.title: Step 4: View Training Data
docs.text:
Training pairs are stored in training_data/:

{

    "prompt": "Explain Docker volumes",

    "chosen": "Docker volumes persist data...",

    "rejected": "Volumes in Docker are..."

}

docs.title: Step 5: Run GRPO Training
docs.text:
Admin Only

Training requires admin access.

# Trigger training

curl -X POST http://192.168.2.103/swarm/v1/training/trigger \

    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \

    -d '{"owner_id": "user-123"}'

The MarsRL loop uses your preference data to fine-tune response quality.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
graph LR

    A[User Feedback] --> B[Training Data]

    B --> C[GRPO Optimizer]

    C --> D[Updated Reward Model]

    D --> E[Better Responses]

    E --> A

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• User Guide: Training  full training reference

  • Architecture: MarsRL  how MarsRL works

docs.title: Build a Voice Assistant
docs.text: Set up voice input and output to talk to Memex.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• How to enable voice input (STT)

  • How to enable voice output (TTS)

  • How voice integrates with the agent pipeline

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• A microphone connected to the device running the browser

  • Voice services enabled on the Execution Node

docs.title: Step 1: Verify Voice Services
docs.text:
# Check Whisper (STT) is running

curl http://192.168.2.101:9000/health



# Check Piper (TTS) is running

curl http://192.168.2.101:5500/health

docs.title: Step 2: Enable Voice in the UI
docs.text:
In the Hive UI (http://192.168.2.103/):

  1. Click the microphone icon in the chat input area

  2. Grant browser microphone permission when prompted

  3. Speak your message

The audio is sent to Whisper for transcription, then processed as text.

docs.title: Step 3: Enable Voice Output
docs.text:
Agent responses can be spoken aloud:

  1. Open Settings in the UI

  2. Enable Text-to-Speech

  3. Select a voice (Piper offers multiple voices)

Responses will be both displayed as text and played as audio.

docs.title: Step 4: Try a Conversation
docs.text:
Speak naturally:

"What's the weather like?"

"Turn on the living room lights"

"Tell me a joke"

The system handles the full loop: speech ? text ? agent ? text ? speech.

docs.title: Step 5: Adjust Voice Settings
docs.text:
| Setting | Effect |

|---------|--------|

| Voice model | Changes the speaker voice |

| Speed | Adjusts speech rate |

| Auto-listen | Automatically starts listening after TTS finishes |

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
| Issue | Fix |

|-------|-----|

| Microphone not detected | Check browser permissions |

| Poor transcription | Reduce background noise, speak clearly |

| No audio output | Check browser audio permissions and volume |

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• User Guide: Voice  full voice reference

  • Create IoT Automation  combine voice with smart home
